author: @steveharrison7615
text: Just use a big file, it’s helps to keep it flat and level and finish with emery cloth

author: @ZoVoSinc
text: if you take it back too far by a little bit, getting a loose fit, you can electroplate the thickness back.

author: @G58
text: A good way to work on very large diameter disks is to use a wood lathe with the head moved down to the end of the bed whilst mounted on a bench. This allows the disk workpiece to hang in free space beside the bench. The biggest disk I’ve worked on this way was 30” diameter. Thank you for sharing

author: @kgsalvage6306
text: The only problem when doing a larger flywheel or something is it's hard to get it totally true using a grinder or something.  Unless the grinder is stationary.  Solid.  Otherwise,  if the wheel isn't totally round,  it will just follow the same shape as it is.  Hope I'm getting the point across.  I've done it though.  Using a drill press even.

Really wish I’d have thought about something like this, it could have saved quite a lot of time and fuel.  I do appreciate you taking the time to make this video,it may save me in future endeavors.

author: @Dr-Sy
text:
I love your videos, always fun and very interesting, Here let me run this through you if i may, Its an idea for and electro magnetic pulse engine ' Not sure if it would work but the concept is there'.

You basically have the same setup as a 2 cylinder engine that would run on petrol and change some parts. the petrol engine needs a spark plug to ignite the fuel pushing the piston down on number one cylinder, As cylinder one goes down cylinder two comes up and the same thing happens again. (that's how a engine works) My idea is to replace the spark plugs with some strong magnets that are connected to the coil and instead of the spark plug pushing the piston down, You'd have the electro magnet pulse from the coil pulling the piston up and when then piston reaches its peak then electro magnet pulse from the coil two would become electrified pulling up piston 2 making piston one go down. 

So Current from coil to piston one magnet
When piston one reaches TDC current to piston one is shut off
Current to piston two magnet is engaged pulling up piston two
When that reaches TDC it shuts the current off to piston two, repeating the process.

I'm sure this could even be built with mostly plastic/3d printed and would be pretty silent as there would be no pops and bangs and we know electro magnets can pick up a car. you could make the entire thing out of plastic/3d printed parts and just have the magnets and small steal thin plates that can easily be drawn to a magnetic field.

I hope you get what i mean.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I get it mate - to my reading it looks very similar to a solenoid engine?

replies.author: @Dr-Sy
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering yes but instead of the small coils they use, You would use one in which you'd find in a car that produces the same current to the spark plug.

like have a 12v battery to a electronics board for timing the coil to send current a distributor to distribute to piston one and two, for a short period the coil would turn the magnet into a 40,000volt electro magnet. which in turn pulls up a piston that's inside a cylinder. the other end of the piston you have a crank and on the crank we have to conrods, the crank could then be connected back to the battery for sustainability or to drive something, The faster the switching of current the faster the motor would turn.

author: @jpsimon206
text: This could never hold up to tool pressure if you used an actual cutter. Putting aside all the rigidity issues, it could theoretically work if you added a third caster to each assembly, essentially building a pair of steady rests. The part would just jump out of the cradle otherwise

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sorry mate - I don't really understand why folks say something couldn't work when they have just seen it work? - I get you point about tool pressure but to be honest I think that is a broad scope response and in reality it would be a matter of the tool, pressure applied, the type material being cut and the mass of the material being rotated

replies.author: @jpsimon206
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I don't think this is fair. I'm not a troll, my comment included a suggestion for how it could work with a traditional lathe tool. In fairness, you did not actually demonstrate the technique, simply told us how you did it. I think my comment clearly was referring to traditional lathe tooling, and I went the extra step to not only explain my opinion, but to suggest a modification that would make it work in many situations and on many parts. Perhaps I was mistaken, but I was under the impression that the comment section was for the sake of discussion. I found the idea interesting beyond your particular use case. You set my brain to thinking why industrial lathes do not have a similar feature. I proposed a method by which someone without a large lathe could still do large work. Isn't the point of communication to learn from one another?

author: @totherarf
text: When you start getting close you can swap the grinder for some emery cloth! We get it in 20mm wide strips. Rip a suitable length off and hold each end with the middle doing the work. A cheap set of vernier gauges works well for a few quid ;o)
